text: In a p-n-p transistor working as a common base amplifier, when the current gain is 0.96 and emitter current is 7.2 mA, the base current is
explanation:
In common base amplifier for p-n-p transistor,
current gain, $\alpha = 0.96$
Emitter current, $I_E = 7.2$ mA
We know that,
$\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E}$
$\Rightarrow 0.96 = \frac{I_C}{7.2}$
$\Rightarrow I_C = 0.96 \times 7.2 = 6.912$ mA
Base current,
$I_B = I_E - I_C = 7.2 - 6.912 = 0.288$ mA $\approx 0.29$ mA.
name: Questions Item 1

text: In a photodiode, the value of the emf produced by a monochromatic light beam is directly proportional to
options: the barrier potential at p-n junction
the intensity of light falling on the photodiode
the frequency of light falling on the photodiode
the voltage applied at the p-n junction
explanation: In a photodiode, the value of the emf produced by monochromatic light beam is directly proportional to the intensity of light falling on the photodiode.
name: Questions Item 2

text: In the following circuit, the equivalent resistance between A and B is
explanation:
Given figure,
In the figure, diodes 1 and 2 both are in reverse bias and in reverse bias, diode does not conduct, so we may replace diodes 1 and 2 as open circuit (OC).
Here, resistors of resistances $8\ \Omega$, $2\ \Omega$ and $6\ \Omega$ are in series. So, equivalent resistance between points A and B
$R_{AB} = 8 + 2 + 6 = 16\ \Omega$.
name: Questions Item 3

text: A Zener diode is made by
options: heavily doping both p- and n-sides of a p-n junction diode
heavily doping the p-side and lightly doping the n-side of a p-n junction diode
lightly doping the p-side and heavily doping the n-side of a p-n junction diode
lightly doping both p- and n-sides of a p-n junction diode
explanation: A Zener diode is made by heavily doping both p- and n-sides of the junction. Because of heavily doping, a very thin depletion region is formed between the p- and n-sides, and hence the electric field of the junction is extremely high even for a small reverse bias voltage.
name: Questions Item 4

text: For an LED to emit light in visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum, it can have energy band gap in the range of
explanation:
Maximum and minimum energy of photon in visible light,
$E_{\text{max}} = 2.88$ eV $\approx 3$ eV
$E_{\text{min}} = 1.65$ eV $\approx 1.7$ eV
So, the correct option matches between the energy 1.7 eV to 3 eV.
name: Questions Item 5

text: In the following common-emitter circuit, $\beta = 100$ and $V_{CE} = 7$ V. If $V_{BE}$ is negligible, then the base current is
explanation:
Applying KVL,
$-15 + 2K I_C + V_{CE} = 0$
$\Rightarrow I_C = 4$ mA
Common-emitter current gain, $\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B}$
or $I_B = \frac{I_C}{\beta} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{100} = 0.04$ mA.
name: Questions Item 6

text: Three amplifiers with voltage gains 10, 20 and 30 are connected in series. If the input signal has a peak value of 1 mV then the peak value of the output voltage is
explanation:
Total gain of three amplifiers connected in series is given by
$A = A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3 = 10 \times 20 \times 30 = 6000$
But, $A = \frac{V_o}{V_m} \Rightarrow V_o = A \times V_m = 6000 \times 10^{-3} = 6$ V.
name: Questions Item 7

text: In the circuit given, the current through Zener diode is
explanation:
Potential drop across Zener diode is constant. So, applying KVL in the circuit.
$15 = I R_1 + V_z \Rightarrow 15 = I(500) + 10$
$\Rightarrow I = \frac{(15-10)}{500} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ A = 10 mA
There is potential drop of 10 V across the 1500 Ω resistor.
So, $I_1 = \frac{10}{1500} = 6.66$ mA
Therefore, the current through the zener diode,
$I_z = I - I_1 = 10 - 6.66 = 3.33$ mA.
name: Questions Item 8

text: The value of $Y_1$ and $Y_2$, respectively in the following logic circuit if both A and B are 1.
explanation:
The logical circuit can be explained as,
$Y_1 = 0$ and $Y_2 = 1$.
name: Questions Item 9

text: If the diodes are ideal in the circuit given below, then the current through the cell is
explanation:
Diode, $D_1$ = reverse biased (OFF) and diode, $D_2$ = forward biased (ON). Because diodes are ideal, so voltage drop across $D_2$ is zero.
Effective resistance, $R = 3 + 2 + 3 + 2 = 10\ \Omega$
Current through the cell,
$I = V / R = 20 / 10 = 2$ A.
name: Questions Item 10

text: In n-p-n transistor, the values of $R_1$ and $R_2$ are respectively.
explanation:
In given circuit, $V_{CC} = i_B R_B + V_{BE}$
$R_B = R_1 = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{i_B}$
As, $i_B = \frac{i_C}{\beta} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{50} = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ A
$R_1 = \frac{8 - 0.6}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 74$ kΩ
By KVL in closed collector loop, we get
$V_{CC} = i_C R_L + V_{CE}$
$R_L = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE}}{i_C} = \frac{8 - 3}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0$ kΩ
So, $R_2 = R_L = 1.0$ kΩ.
name: Questions Item 11

text: The two combinations of NAND gates shown in the figure are equivalent to
explanation: The combinations are equivalent to OR gate.

text: If the diodes are ideal in the circuit given below, then the current through the cell is
explanation:
Diode, $D_1$ = reverse biased (OFF) and diode, $D_2$ = forward biased (ON). Because diodes are ideal, so voltage drop across $D_2$ is zero.
Effective resistance, $R = 3 + 2 + 3 + 2 = 10\ \Omega$
Current through the cell,
$I = V / R = 20 / 10 = 2$ A.
name: Questions Item 13

text: In the n-p-n transistor circuit, the value of $\beta$ varies between
explanation:
Given, in a transistor current gain,
$\alpha = \frac{20}{21}$ and $\frac{100}{101}$
Common emitter transistor DC current gain, $\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$
Case I If $\alpha = \frac{20}{21}$, then $\beta = 20$
Case II $\alpha = \frac{100}{101}$, then $\beta = 100$
Value of $\beta$ varies between $\beta \in [20, 100]$.
name: Questions Item 14

text: In a p-type semiconductor the donor level is at 50 meV above the valence band. To produce one electron, the maximum wavelength of light photon required is
explanation:
p-type semiconductor donor energy level,
$E = 50$ meV = $50 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ V
Planck's constant, $h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34}$ Js
speed of light in vacuum, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
Now, for the maximum wavelength of light photon's required ($\lambda$).
According to the Planck's quantum theory,
$E = h\nu \Rightarrow E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$
Putting the given values, we get
$50 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{\lambda}$
$\lambda = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{50 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 24.75\ \mu$m.
name: Questions Item 15
